coredata.dc:title: Strongly coupled NH2NH-modified high crystallinity Graphene quantum dots/Carbon Nitride for efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

Zhu, Xianglin

Zeng, Guixin
Miao, Honghai
Mo, Zhao
Hossain, Md Shouquat

Wang, Liang
Xu, Hui
coredata.dc:description: For the application of photocatalysis, the development of noble metal-free cocatalysts is crucial. Here, a simple molecular fusion method was employed to create high crystallinity GQDs with the –NHNH2 modification, which were then used as an effective metal-free cocatalyst in the photocatalytic H2 evolution reaction. The modified –NHNH2 Lewis base active sites, the quick charge separation channels, and the improved electric conductivity allowed the optimized NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN sample to exhibit an H2 evolution rate of 204.2 μmol g−1 h−1, which is an 80-fold greater activity than that of pure PUCN. This work will provide insights into the design of metal-free cocatalysts for photocatalytic H2 evolution applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: High crystallinity Graphene quantum dots
Lewis base active sites
Photocatalytic H2 evolution
Cocatalyst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22005123 , 21776118 , 22178152 , 21878134 ), Jiangsu Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2021K382C , 2021K079A ), Jiangsu University Foundation ( 20JDG16 ), Jiangsu Funds for Distinguished Young Scientists ( BK20190045 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2021M691305 ), Jiangsu Agricultural Science and Technology Independent Innovation Fund ( CX(21)3067 ), and the Postgraduate High-Tech Research Key Laboratory of Zhenjiang ( SS2018002 ), the authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) FTIR spectra of PUCN, 1 wt%, 3 wt%, and 5 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN. (c) Raman spectra of NHNH2-GQDs, 1 wt%, 3 wt%, and 5 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN.
(a–c) TEM images of 3 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN. (d) TEM and (e) HRTEM images of NHNH2-GQDs, and (f–i) mapping images of 3 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN.
XPS spectra of PUCN and 3 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN: (a) Total survey, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s.
(a) Photocatalytic H2 evolution over PUCN, 1 wt%, 3 wt%, and 5 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN. And (b) Photocatalytic stability test over 3 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN.

(a) UV–vis of PUCN, 1 wt%, 3 wt%, and 5 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN. (b) PL spectra of PUCN and 3 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN. (c) Transient photocurrent spectra and (d) EIS spectra of PUCN, 1 wt%, 3 wt%, and 5 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN.
Schematic illustration of the proposed charge behavior during photocatalytic reaction.

head.ce:title: Strongly coupled NH2NH-modified high crystallinity Graphene quantum dots/Carbon Nitride for efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

State Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Drug Discovery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanochemistry and Nanobiology, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nanochemistry and Nanobiology, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For the application of photocatalysis, the development of noble metal-free cocatalysts is crucial. Here, a simple molecular fusion method was employed to create high crystallinity GQDs with the –NHNH2 modification, which were then used as an effective metal-free cocatalyst in the photocatalytic H2 evolution reaction. The modified –NHNH2 Lewis base active sites, the quick charge separation channels, and the improved electric conductivity allowed the optimized NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN sample to exhibit an H2 evolution rate of 204.2 μmol g−1 h−1, which is an 80-fold greater activity than that of pure PUCN. This work will provide insights into the design of metal-free cocatalysts for photocatalytic H2 evolution applications.

ce:section.ce:para: In the energy field, the use of solar energy to produce clean energy has been a popular topic. Since the TiO2 electrodes was initially used by Fujishima and his coworkers for photoelectrochemical water splitting, photocatalysis technology as one potential strategy for green hydrogen production has gained considerable interest [1]. Until now, the design of highly active photocatalysts is still the vital factor for the practical application of photocatalysis technology [2,3]. A number of classic semiconductor materials, such as ZnO, CdS, TaON, BiVO4, were found and developed as photocatalysts for contaminants degradation and solar energy conversion [4–12]. However, the existing problem of inappropriate bandgap structure, poor chemical stability, and the lack of active sites result in low solar energy conversion efficiency. Recently, graphic Carbon Nitride (g-C3N4) has been as a famous catalyst in the photocatalytic field due to the merits of easy synthesis, good chemical stability, visible light response, and adjustable molecule structure [13–18]. However, the application of g-C3N4 is still a challenge for the practical application because of the lack of active site, the rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers, and the high overpotentials of water splitting [19–23].

According to the reports of previous publications, several strategies of morphology engineering, element doping, heterojunction building, and cocatalyst modification are proven effective methods to enhance optical absorption qualities and speeding up charge separation [20,24–27]. The loading of cocatalysts is crucial in the water splitting progress because the kinetic rates of H2O reduction and oxidation progresses are severely restricted due to their high overpotentials [28–30]. In addition to serving as active sites in H2 evolution and O2 evolution processes, the modification of co-catalysts can also create a heterojunction interface that can promote the separation of photogenerated carriers [31]. The most common used cocatalyst in the photocatalytic H2 evolution reaction is platinum, a valuable metal element. However, the prohibitive cost of platinum restricts its practical application. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a new type of carbon material, have drawn a lot of interest, particularly in the photocatalytic field attributed to the π-plasmon induced great optical absorption and the outstanding photoluminescence emission resulting from the structure or edge defects [32,33]. Additionally, GQDs have the potentials to work as effective photo-excited electron transfer centers, enhancing the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers.

Here, NH2NH-modified high crystallinity GQDs were constructed via a simple molecular fusion method. The prepared NH2NH-modified high crystallinity GQDs (NH2NH-GQDs) have high crystallinity cores and –NHNH2 functionalized edges sites. Owing to the good crystallinity of the cores, the prepared NH2NH-GQDs have excellent electric conductivity and can catch the photoelectrons from g-C3N4. Meanwhile, the grafted –NHNH2 group at the edge sites as Lewis base sites will anchor the H+ (Lewis acid) in the solution [34–36]. As a result, the quick electron transfer and abundant active Lewis base enable the effective photocatalytic H2 evolution process. In addition, due to the 0D/2D structure composed of quantum dots and porous ultrathin nanosheets, the dispersion of NH2NH-GQDs on g-C3N4 is uniform, and the formed heterojunction interface contact is very close. This will provide effective channels for the fast transfer of photogenerated carriers, therefore further enhancing the photocatalytic activity. As a consequence, an 80-fold increase in activity was achieved using the optimized NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN photocatalyst without modifying the noble metal cocatalyst.

The crystal structure of NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN was verified by XRD, Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) Spectroscopy, and Raman spectra, as shown in Fig. 1 . In Fig. 1a, the peaks located at around 13.1° and 27.4° were found to become weaker as the amount of NHNH2-GQDs increases. As is known, these two peaks are the characteristic diffraction peaks of π-π interlayer stacking structure coming from the long-range atomic with a distance of 0.326 nm. The weakening of peak intensity can indicate the embedding of NHNH2-GQDs into the in-planes of PUCN. In the FTIR results (Fig. 1b), the signals of CN, C–N, and triazine units have no obvious change after the loading of NHNH2-GQDs, proving the chemical structure of PUCN is not destroyed. While the signal at round 3068 cm-1 is enhanced, indicating the existence of N–H with the loading amount increase of NHNH2-GQDs. The successful introduction of NNHN2-GQDs can be further verified by the Raman spectrum, two typical peaks at 1474 and 1367 cm-1 are observed, which are assigned to the crystalline G band and the disordered D band of NNHN2-GQDs, respectively. The above two weak peaks can also be found in NNHN2-GQDs/PUCN, which proves the existence of NNHN2-GQDs [37–39].

The morphologies of as prepared catalyst are investigated using a Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM). From the TEM images of 3 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN in Fig. 2 a–c and Figs. S1a and b, we can clearly observe distributed holes in the PUCN; meanwhile, the ultrathin structure of PUCN was also verified by the results in Fig. 2b and c. The uniformly dispersed NHNH2-GQDs were verified in the High-Resolution TEM (HRTEM) results, as shown in Fig. 2d and Figs. S1c and d. In Fig. 2e, one clear distance of 0.2 nm was discovered, indicating the high crystalline graphite structure of NHNH2-GQDs. The above TEM results can confirm the construction of a 0D/2D structure composed of NHNH2-GQDs and PUCN. This distinctive structure can preserve light-capture capability while maximizing the utilization of the active site. Fig. 2f–i are the mapping images of 3 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN, which can indicate the uniform distribution of C and N in the sample.

By examining the binding energy data from the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), as shown in Fig. 3 , the alterations of the chemical compositions before and after NHNH2-GQDs loading were also investigated. In the complete surveys of the samples, the signs of C, N, and O were discovered. From the C 1s spectrum of NHNH2-GQDs in Fig. 3a, we can infer that the GQDs contain significant amounts of –OH and –NH2 because of the obvious existence of C–OH (288.3 eV) and C–N (286.3 eV) signals, which are substantially different from the C 1s spectrum of PUCN. Due to the low concentration of NHNH2-GQDs in the composite, the typical NHNH2-GQD peaks were covered by the 3 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN. In the high-resolution spectrum of N 1s, the signals at around 399.6 eV and N-401.8 eV correspond to the functional groups of –NHNH2 and N–C, indicating the chemical bonding of –NHNH2 -modified GQDs. The PUCN and 3 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN showed similar N 1s spectrums because of the low loading content of GQDs. In the O 1s spectrum, the peaks of 531.5 eV and 533.1 eV are the signals of O–H and O–N coming from the partial oxidation of surface –NH2 [40]. The XPS results confirm the successful modification of GQDs with –NHNH2 groups, which can act as active centers for photocatalytic H2 evolution.

The photocatalytic H2 evolution performance of as-prepared samples was tested as shown in Fig. 4 . The H2 evolution rate of pristine PUCN is only 12.2 μM g−1in 5 h. After loading NHNH2-GQDs, photocatalytic H2 evolution activity was improved. The H2 evolution rates with loading 1 wt%, 3 wt%, and 5 wt % NHNH2-GQDs were 74.1, 204.2, and 133.7 μmol g−1, respectively. The 3 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN showed the highest H2 evolution rate, which is about 84 times that of pristine PUCN. It was proven that the NHNH2-GQDs were one kind of effective cocatalyst for photocatalytic H2 evolution. When the loading amount of NHNH2-GQDs was over 3%, the H2 evolution activity was restrained because of the weakened light absorption efficiency. The stability of the catalyst was evaluated through cyclic tests as shown in Fig. 4b. No obvious activity decay was found, proving the good stability of NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN. The good stability of the NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN catalyst can be attributed to the advantages of the formed 0D/2D heterojunction, which helps the desperation and contact of quantum dots on the porous ultrathin carbon nitride. Besides, the good stability of NHNH2-GQDs is also one important factor that makes the stable H2 evolution performance.

The optical absorption capabilities of the catalysts were assessed in order to determine the mechanism behind the increased activity, as shown in Fig. 5 a. The PUCN showed an absorption edge at around 450 nm. The NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN sample showed stronger light responses in the range of visible light as the concentration of NHNH2-GQDs increased. The enhanced light responses will help the photocatalytic performance. Additionally, the results of steady-state photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were also tested, as shown in Fig. 5b. The 3 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN sample showed a weaker PL intensity than the PUCN sample, indicating that the introduction of NHNH2-GQDs reduced the recombination of photo-generated carriers and improve the separation efficiency of the generated carriers [41]. The faster charge separation ability of NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN samples is further discovered through photocurrent response and EIS spectra, as shown in Fig. 5c and d. Compared with PUCN, the charge transfer resistance of 3 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN is lower. The result is consistent with the changes in the photocurrent test, and it is also consistent with the result that 3 wt% NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN has higher photocatalytic activity than PUCN. The lower transfer resistance means a faster transfer efficiency of the carriers. The introduction of NHNH2-GQDs further accelerated the speed of electron transfer and increased active centers. The existence of NHNH2-GQDs is beneficial to lower the charge transfer resistance, providing more effective photoelectron–hole separation, and thus enhancing the photocatalytic properties of PUCN. The Mott-Schottky characterization was carried out to verify the bandgap structure as shown in Fig. S5. From the results, the flat band potential of PUCN is about −1.20 V. After loading the NHNH2-GQDs, the NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN has a flat band potential of −1.0. The lower flat band potential can indicate the transfer of electrons from to NHNH2-GQDs.

Based on the aforementioned research, we give a possible photocatalytic mechanism, which can explain the working mechanism of the NHNH2-GQDs as shown in Fig. 6 . First, a particular type of porous, ultrathin carbon nitride with rich holes and an ultrathin structure was created. Then, high crystallinity GQDs with –NHNH2 functionalized edges were deposited onto the porous ultrathin carbon nitride. Due to the formed 0D/2D heterojunction, a close contact between the carbon nitride and GQDs will be built and work as effective electron transfer channels for the electron migration. Besides, taking advantage to the good crystallinity of good NHNH2-GQDs, the electrons will quickly diffuse to the cocatalysts/water interface. Furthermore, the abundant –NHNH2 sites acting as Lewis base sites can attach the H+ (Lewis acid) in the solution which will lower the H2 evolution barrier and support the efficient photocatalytic H2 evolution progress.

To boost the charge transfer and construct active sites, the high crystallinity GQDs with modified –NHNH2 were prepared as a cocatalyst to promote the photocatalytic H2 evolution activity of carbon nitride. Due to the close interface and the abundant of active –NHNH2 sites, the optimized NHNH2-GQDs/PUCN catalyst displayed a H2 evolution rate of 1021 μmol g−1 in 5 h, which is over 80 times that of the pristine PUCN. Our work will provide new insights into the design of a highly efficient metal-free photocatalyst for the H2 evolution reaction.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22005123, 21776118, 22178152, 21878134), Jiangsu Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021K382C, 2021K079A), Jiangsu University Foundation (20JDG16), Jiangsu Funds for Distinguished Young Scientists (BK20190045), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M691305), Jiangsu Agricultural Science and Technology Independent Innovation Fund (CX(21)3067), and the Postgraduate High-Tech Research Key Laboratory of Zhenjiang (SS2018002), the authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
